name: Grallariidae
description: Antpittas are plump, short-tailed, long-legged passerines that hop through the undergrowth of Neotropical forests. They are known for their loud, haunting songs and secretive behavior.

children.name: Grallaria
children.description: Grallaria is the largest genus of antpittas, containing medium to large species found primarily in the Andes.

children.name: Grallaria squamigera
children.description: The undulated antpitta is a large, striking antpitta with wavy black-and-buff barring across its underparts. It inhabits humid montane forests of the Andes from Colombia to Bolivia.

children.name: Grallaria ruficapilla
children.description: The chestnut-crowned antpitta has a distinctive bright chestnut cap and is found in the Andes of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. Its loud, trilling song is a characteristic sound of cloud forests.

children.name: Grallaria quitensis
children.description: The tawny antpitta is a uniform brownish-olive species restricted to high-altitude paramo and elfin forests of the northern Andes. It is one of the highest-elevation antpitta species.

children.name: Grallaria rufula
children.description: The rufous antpitta is a small, warm-rufous species found in Andean cloud forests from Venezuela to Bolivia. Recent genetic studies have split this species into several distinct cryptic species.

children.name: Grallaria gigantea
children.description: The giant antpitta is the largest antpitta species, reaching up to 24 cm in length, with dark brown upperparts and heavily streaked white underparts. It is found in humid montane forests of Colombia and Ecuador. Its deep, resonant song is a powerful series of hooting notes.

children.name: Grallaria excelsa
children.description: The great antpitta is a large, robust antpitta with olive-brown upperparts, a grey crown, and heavily streaked whitish underparts. It is endemic to the cloud forests of the Venezuelan Andes. It is considered vulnerable due to habitat loss.

children.name: Grallaria guatimalensis
children.description: The scaled antpitta has distinctive black-edged feathers on its underparts that create a scaled appearance. It ranges from southern Mexico through Central America to the Andes of Peru and Bolivia. It inhabits humid montane forests and cloud forests.

children.name: Grallaria hypoleuca
children.description: The white-bellied antpitta has brown upperparts, a white belly, and a greyish breast with fine streaking. It is found in the Andes of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. It inhabits the understory of humid montane forests.

children.name: Grallaria rufocinerea
children.description: The bicolored antpitta has rich rufous underparts contrasting with dark olive-brown upperparts. It is found in the cloud forests of Colombia and Ecuador. Its song is a series of slow, deep hoots.

children.name: Grallaria nuchalis
children.description: The chestnut-naped antpitta has a distinctive chestnut nape and crown, olive-brown upperparts, and grey underparts. It is found in the Andes of Colombia, Ecuador, and Peru. It forages on the forest floor for insects and other invertebrates.

children.name: Grallaria flavotincta
children.description: The yellow-breasted antpitta has a bright yellow breast and belly, olive-brown upperparts, and a grey crown. It is found in the cloud forests of Colombia and Ecuador. Its song is a slow, melancholic series of descending whistles.

children.name: Grallaria bangsi
children.description: The Santa Marta antpitta is endemic to the Sierra Nevada de Santa Marta in Colombia, where it inhabits high-altitude cloud forests. It has olive-brown upperparts, a greyish breast, and a white belly. It is considered near threatened due to its restricted range.

children.name: Grallaricula
children.description: Grallaricula is a genus of small antpittas, among the tiniest members of the family, found in humid montane forests.

children.name: Grallaricula flavirostris
children.description: The ochre-breasted antpitta is a tiny antpitta with a warm ochre breast and a narrow pale eye-ring. It ranges from Costa Rica to Bolivia in foothill and montane forests.

children.name: Hylopezus
children.description: A genus of medium-sized antpittas with relatively long bills and distinctive breast spotting, found in lowland and foothill forests of Central and South America.

children.name: Hylopezus perspicillatus
children.description: The speckled antpitta has olive-brown upperparts, a white throat, and heavily spotted whitish underparts. It ranges from Honduras through Central America to the Pacific slope of Colombia and Ecuador. It inhabits lowland and foothill forests, foraging on the ground in dense undergrowth.

children.name: Myrmothera
children.description: A genus of stocky, short-tailed antpittas found in the Amazon basin and the Guiana Shield. They are among the most terrestrial of the antpittas, with strong legs adapted for hopping through the forest understory.

children.name: Myrmothera campanisona
children.description: The thrush-like antpitta has brown upperparts, a white throat, and spotted buffy underparts, resembling a small thrush. It is widespread across the Amazon basin in lowland rainforests. Its song is a series of clear, descending whistles that accelerate at the end.
